%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 1.2-4 Combined Operations

> **Objective:** Master the two valid ways to build x(at - b) from x(t), read the correct shift amount in each route, and avoid the most common exam trap.

---

Consider x(2t - 6). At first glance it looks like a simple shift-by-6 plus a compression. But that reading is only half right — and the half that is wrong is exactly what exams test.

This section covers three things: identifying the correct operation order, reading the effective shift without error, and avoiding the trap of always saying "shift by b" regardless of what comes first.

### KEY MEMORY RULE

The signal x(at - b) can be built in exactly two valid ways:
- **Shift first by b, then scale by a**, or
- **Scale first by a, then shift by b/a**

Both routes reach the same final expression. The shift amount is different in each route.

#### Warning

If a < 0, time reversal is automatically included in the scaling step — do not ignore the sign.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. 1.9](/figures/page-077-fig__1_9-1.png)
*This textbook figure shows the core visual meaning of time reversal: points at negative time reflect to corresponding positive-time locations.*

## 1. How to Read x(at - b)

There are exactly two correct operation sequences for constructing x(at - b) from x(t). Both are valid; neither is a shortcut.

**Sequence 1 — Shift First, Then Scale:**

Start from x(t). Shift by b to obtain x(t - b). Then replace t with at to obtain x(at - b). The shift amount in this route is b.

**Sequence 2 — Scale First, Then Shift:**

Start from x(t). Scale first to obtain x(at). Then shift that result by b/a to obtain x[a(t - b/a)] = x(at - b). The shift amount in this route is b/a.

These two sequences produce identical final expressions, but the intermediate shift amount is different in each.

### COMMON MISTAKE

If you scale first, the shift is **not b**; it is **b/a**. Applying a shift of b after scaling is the single most common error on combined-operation problems.

$$x(at-b)=x\left[a\left(t-\frac{b}{a}\right)\right]$$
*This identity is the exam-saving rewrite: it lets you read the scale-first interpretation directly, where scaling happens first and the subsequent shift amount is b/a, not b.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*📊 Two valid routes to x(at - b): the left path shifts first (shift amount = b), the right path scales first (shift amount = b/a). Both reach the same final expression. Warning at bottom: if a < 0, reversal is included.*
![Chart](/generated/chart-1777138914372-i4sct.png)

## 2. Fast Read for x(2t - 6)

Apply both methods to the concrete signal x(2t - 6).

**Method A — Shift First:**

Delay x(t) by 6 to get x(t - 6). Then time-compress by factor 2 to get x(2t - 6). Here the shift amount is 6 and it is applied before compression.

**Method B — Scale First (recommended for exams):**

First compress x(t) by factor 2 to get x(2t). Then delay by b/a = 6/2 = 3, because x(2t - 6) = x[2(t - 3)]. The effective shift in this reading is 3, not 6.

### EXAM TIP

Factor the coefficient of t immediately: x(2t - 6) = x[2(t - 3)]. The number inside the parentheses after factoring is the effective delay under the scale-first view.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Students see x(2t - 6) and say "shift right by 6." Under the scale-first reading, the effective shift is **3**. Saying "shift by 6" is only correct if you are describing the shift-first route before compression is applied.

$$x(2t-6)=x\left[2\left(t-3\right)\right]$$
*Rewriting the argument this way reveals the scale-first interpretation immediately: compress by factor 2 first, then shift right by 3 — not by 6.*

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Two valid orders: shift-by-b then scale-by-a, or scale-by-a then shift-by-b/a.
- Scale first means the shift amount changes to b/a, not b — this is the exam trap.
- If a < 0, time reversal is automatically part of the scaling step.

*In the next section we will continue using signal transformations to read and sketch signals faster.*
